refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) during equipment operation.
Store throughput measurement data with sufficient fidelity and temporal resolution for analysis.
Report throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous measurement of material volume or mass crossing a defined control plane, integrate over time, and report cumulative throughput and rate metrics.
DP2: Periodically sample material quantity at fixed time intervals, calculate rate from successive samples, and extrapolate to report throughput over evaluation period.
DP3: Measure material mass or volume at process entry and exit points, compute net change per unit time, and report as throughput rate.
DP4: Instrument material handling equipment (e.g., conveyor, hopper) with load or displacement sensors; derive flow rate from equipment state transitions and report integrated throughput.
DP5: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., mass + volume, entry + exit points) with cross-validation logic to report throughput with confidence bounds.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous measurement of material volume or mass crossing a defined control plane, integrate over time, and report cumulative throughput and rate metrics.

potential_sources: Control plane definition must be independent of material type and process variability to avoid re-design when feedstock changes.
Measurement modality (mass vs. volume) should be decoupled from reporting logic; selection driven by constraint analysis, not reporting algorithm.
Integration and reporting functions should be independent modules to allow independent testing and recalibration.
Acceptance criteria threshold (productivity_target) must be parameterized, not hard-coded, to decouple measurement from policy.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low coupling because it separates concerns: (1) physical measurement at a single, well-defined plane, (2) temporal integration, (3) reporting. Each can be validated independently. DP2 and DP3 introduce sampling bias and multi-point measurement complexity respectively. DP4 couples measurement to equipment type, reducing generality. DP5 adds redundancy but increases complexity without addressing the core FR.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: A single, accessible control plane exists where material flow can be measured without significant process disruption.
ASSUMPTION: Material properties (density, particle size distribution) are sufficiently stable during evaluation period, or measurement modality is insensitive to variation.
ASSUMPTION: Measurement instrumentation can achieve accuracy and repeatability consistent with productivity_target tolerance (not yet quantified).
RISK: If control plane is inaccessible or measurement introduces unacceptable process friction, DP1 becomes infeasible; fallback to DP3 or DP4 required.
RISK: Temporal integration accuracy depends on sampling frequency and sensor response time; if not specified, reported throughput may systematically under- or over-estimate true rate.

RISK: Acceptance criteria references 'representative operations' without defining operational envelope (load, ambient conditions, material variability); measurement validation scope may be insufficient.
RISK: Worksite safety plan may impose constraints on sensor placement or measurement method not yet surfaced; early coordination required.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) acceptable measurement modality (mass, volume, or both) and tolerance on reported throughput (e.g., ±5% of productivity_target); (2) definition of 'representative operations' (material type range, process load range, duration); (3) location and accessibility of control plane; (4) integration period (instantaneous rate, rolling average, cumulative over evaluation period). Establish a DP-to-IM mapping plan that assigns measurement acquisition, integration, and reporting to distinct, independently testable modules. Conduct early safety and operability review with worksite stakeholders to confirm control plane feasibility and identify constraints on sensor placement or measurement method.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all physical sensing, actuation, and real-time measurement acquisition including flow rate sensors, control plane monitoring, integration logic, and local metric computation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle reports cumulative throughput and instantaneous rate metrics to external consumers (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives measurement configuration, calibration, and reporting frequency directives from NGHE Solution or Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle may emit flow anomaly alerts if rate deviates beyond safe thresholds (optional, if safety-critical).

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Vehicle owns physical sensors, control plane definition, continuous measurement hardware, and local integration logic. Cumulative throughput and rate computation are naturally resident. Minor diffusion: reporting interface (Telemetry) crosses boundary, but this is expected and well-defined.

Poor fit. NGHE Solution is a higher-level orchestration/planning layer. Placing instantaneous flow measurement and continuous integration here violates separation of concerns. Sensor hardware and real-time acquisition logic would be orphaned in Vehicle, creating tight coupling and distributed responsibility.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command relay layer. It should consume flow metrics, not own the measurement and integration machinery. Placing DP here would require Teleoperation to manage sensors and real-time computation, violating its intended role.

Poor fit. Safety Services is a policy and alert layer. While it may consume flow rate data to enforce safety constraints, it should not own the measurement acquisition or integration logic. Doing so would conflate measurement responsibility with safety enforcement.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural owner of DP1 because it is the physical system that contains the control plane, hosts the flow sensors, and executes real-time measurement and integration. The DP is fundamentally a Vehicle capability: acquiring and reporting its own throughput metrics. External modules (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) consume these metrics via the Telemetry interface without owning the measurement machinery.
containment_risks: If Vehicle is a passive data container without embedded computation, flow integration logic may need to reside in NGHE Solution or a dedicated measurement service, weakening Vehicle ownership. Mitigation: ensure Vehicle has sufficient onboard processing capability or a local edge controller.

If multiple external systems require different flow metric formats or sampling rates, the Telemetry interface may become complex. Mitigation: define a canonical Telemetry schema and let consumers adapt.

If flow sensors are not physically integrated into Vehicle (e.g., external measurement rig), ownership ambiguity may arise. Mitigation: clarify whether sensors are Vehicle-resident or external; if external, create a dedicated Measurement Module and revise IM boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies measurable outputs (cumulative throughput and instantaneous rate in volumetric or mass units per unit time). The Vehicle IM boundary owns the physical sensors, control plane, and integration logic needed to acquire and compute these metrics. Verification can be performed by instrumenting the Vehicle with calibrated flow sensors and validating reported metrics against ground truth during representative operations. The acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' is directly traceable to the Telemetry interface output.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated entirely to the Vehicle IM, which is the appropriate owner. The Vehicle's scope explicitly includes 'flow rate sensors, control plane monitoring, integration logic, and local metric computation.' External modules (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) consume metrics via the Telemetry interface without owning measurement machinery. Ownership boundaries are well-defined and non-overlapping.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP correctly addresses the FR. DP1 specifies a complete solution: (1) acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous measurement at a defined control plane, (2) integrate over time to compute cumulative throughput, and (3) report both cumulative and rate metrics. This directly satisfies the FR requirement to 'enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.' The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe sensor type or integration algorithm) and is implementable. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: Conditional risk: If Vehicle lacks onboard processing capability (e.g., is a passive data container), integration logic may diffuse to NGHE Solution or a dedicated edge service. Mitigation: confirm Vehicle has sufficient compute resources or a local controller to execute real-time integration. If not, revise IM boundary to include a dedicated Measurement Service module and update ownership.

Interface complexity risk: If multiple external consumers (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) require different flow metric formats, sampling rates, or update frequencies, the Telemetry interface may become a diffusion point. Mitigation: define a canonical Telemetry schema with versioning and let consumers adapt via local transformation layers.

Sensor integration risk: If flow sensors are not physically integrated into Vehicle (e.g., external measurement rig or third-party instrument), ownership ambiguity arises. The DP assumes sensors are Vehicle-resident. Mitigation: clarify sensor ownership in design documentation. If sensors are external, create a dedicated Measurement Module IM and revise the Vehicle boundary accordingly.

Minor diffusion (acceptable): The Telemetry interface crosses the Vehicle boundary, but this is expected and well-managed. Vehicle reports metrics; external modules consume them. No bidirectional tight coupling or shared state detected.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. The Vehicle IM achieves a 0.92 containment score with only 0.08 diffusion. The DP is naturally resident in Vehicle because Vehicle owns the physical control plane, hosts the sensors, and executes real-time measurement. The alternative candidates (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all score poorly (0.35–0.45 containment) because they are higher-level orchestration, interface, or policy layers that should consume metrics, not own measurement machinery. The containment hypothesis is sound: Vehicle is the correct owner. Recommended action: proceed with Vehicle as the DP1 owner, but validate the three conditional risks above during detailed design.

recommended_revisions: Confirm Vehicle has onboard processing capability (compute, memory, real-time OS) to execute flow integration logic. If not, introduce a dedicated Measurement Service module and revise the IM boundary.
Define a canonical Telemetry schema for flow metrics (cumulative throughput, instantaneous rate, units, timestamp, confidence/quality flags). Document versioning and backward compatibility strategy.

Clarify sensor ownership and integration: confirm flow sensors are Vehicle-resident and integrated into the control plane. If sensors are external or third-party, create a dedicated Measurement Module IM and update ownership documentation.

Document the control plane definition (spatial and temporal boundaries) for flow measurement. Specify how the control plane is physically realized (e.g., cross-sectional area, measurement point location) to ensure repeatability and traceability.

Add a design note linking the DP to the acceptance criterion: 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' depends on accurate flow measurement and integration. Specify calibration procedures, measurement uncertainty, and validation protocols to ensure the reported metrics meet the productivity target threshold.

decision.justification: The FR-DP-IM gate passes with high confidence (FR-DP logical correctness, DP-IM containment 0.92, clear allocation to Vehicle). However, the gate report identifies three conditional risks that are safety-critical and require decomposition to resolve: (1) Vehicle onboard processing capability is unverified; (2) Telemetry interface schema and multi-consumer format negotiation are unspecified; (3) sensor ownership and physical integration are not yet confirmed. Given the high safety criticality of flow measurement (throughput directly impacts operational safety and productivity targets), these risks must be addressed before proceeding to detailed design. Decomposition into sub-FRs will force explicit resolution of these dependencies and create verifiable acceptance criteria for each risk. This is a justified ZIGZAG_DOWN, not a REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL, because the current FR-DP-IM is logically sound; the decomposition is driven by risk mitigation and architectural clarity, not by logical gaps.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Enable real-time acquisition of instantaneous flow rate at the Vehicle control plane using integrated or Vehicle-resident sensors.
FR1.2: Compute cumulative throughput by integrating instantaneous flow rate over time with specified temporal resolution and numerical accuracy.
FR1.3: Report flow metrics (cumulative throughput and instantaneous rate) via a canonical, versioned Telemetry interface with defined schema, sampling rate, and update frequency.

FR1.4: Ensure Vehicle has sufficient onboard processing capability (compute, memory, real-time OS) to execute flow measurement and integration logic without external dependency or diffusion to NGHE Solution or edge services.

FR1.5: Validate flow measurement accuracy and calibration against ground truth to ensure reported productivity metrics meet the acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' with quantified measurement uncertainty.

revise.why: The Vehicle IM boundary must be explicitly refined to clarify: (a) whether flow sensors are Vehicle-resident or external (if external, a dedicated Measurement Module IM must be introduced); (b) the compute/processing resources available onboard (if insufficient, a Measurement Service IM must be created); (c) the physical and temporal definition of the control plane for flow measurement (location, cross-sectional area, sampling interval). These revisions are IM-level (architectural packaging) and do not require changes to the FR or DP logic, but they are necessary to eliminate ambiguity and diffusion risk before detailed design.

assumptions: Vehicle has onboard processing capability (compute, memory, real-time OS) sufficient to execute flow integration logic in real-time without external dependency.
Flow sensors are physically integrated into the Vehicle and owned by the Vehicle IM boundary.
The control plane for flow measurement is well-defined spatially (location, cross-sectional area) and temporally (sampling interval, integration window).
External consumers (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can adapt to a canonical Telemetry schema via local transformation layers; no bidirectional tight coupling is required.
Measurement uncertainty and calibration procedures are acceptable to meet the productivity_target acceptance criterion.
open_risks: Vehicle Processing Capability Risk (HIGH): If Vehicle lacks onboard compute resources, integration logic will diffuse to NGHE Solution or a dedicated edge service, violating the containment hypothesis. Mitigation: confirm Vehicle compute specifications during detailed design; if insufficient, introduce a Measurement Service IM.

Sensor Ownership Risk (HIGH): If flow sensors are external or third-party instruments not integrated into Vehicle, ownership ambiguity arises and measurement logic may diffuse. Mitigation: clarify sensor integration and ownership in design documentation; if external, create a dedicated Measurement Module IM.

Telemetry Interface Complexity Risk (MEDIUM): Multiple external consumers may require different metric formats, sampling rates, or update frequencies, causing interface diffusion. Mitigation: define a canonical Telemetry schema with versioning and require consumers to adapt via local transformation layers.

Measurement Accuracy Risk (HIGH, Safety-Critical): Flow measurement accuracy directly impacts the acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target.' If measurement uncertainty is too large, the criterion cannot be met. Mitigation: specify calibration procedures, measurement uncertainty bounds, and validation protocols; validate against ground truth during testing.

Control Plane Definition Risk (MEDIUM): The physical and temporal boundaries of the control plane are not yet specified, creating ambiguity in how flow is measured and integrated. Mitigation: define control plane location, cross-sectional area, and sampling interval in detailed design.
evidence_needed: Vehicle compute specifications (processor, memory, real-time OS capability) confirming sufficient resources for real-time flow integration.
Sensor integration documentation: confirm flow sensors are Vehicle-resident, specify sensor type, accuracy class, and physical mounting location.
Control plane definition: specify spatial boundaries (location, cross-sectional area) and temporal boundaries (sampling interval, integration window) for flow measurement.
Canonical Telemetry schema: define flow metric format (cumulative throughput, instantaneous rate, units, timestamp, quality flags), sampling rate, update frequency, and versioning strategy.

Measurement uncertainty analysis: quantify sensor accuracy, integration algorithm error, and total measurement uncertainty; verify that uncertainty is acceptable to meet the productivity_target criterion.
Calibration and validation protocol: specify procedures for calibrating flow sensors, validating reported metrics against ground truth, and documenting measurement traceability.
Design note linking DP to acceptance criterion: document how accurate flow measurement and integration ensure 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' is achievable.
